sourceMovie.title: In the House
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: In the House (2012) stuck because it commits to its comedy premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. François Ozon's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The comedy and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Donnie Darko
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If In the House clicked for you, Donnie Darko (2001) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and mystery, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Love, Simon
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If In the House clicked for you, Love, Simon (2018) is a strong next stop: both trade in comedy and drama, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after In the House?
faq.answer: Try Donnie Darko, Love, Simon, Breakfast at Tiffany's—each shares DNA with In the House while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is In the House?
faq.answer: In the House (2012) is best described as Comedy, Drama, Mystery, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is In the House worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.2/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this comedy lane.
